serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of skinless turkey breast is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one medium breast fillet portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Turkey, breast, meat only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171494)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw skinless turkey breast used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB Foodo values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are preserved from the input record (HPB confidence 0.95) and are consistent with USDA values. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171494.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI testing not performed on pure protein foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for raw skinless turkey breast. Carbohydrate content is 0.14 g per 100 g, which is nutritionally negligible and insufficient to produce a measurable glycaemic response. GI testing is not performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for turkey breast, consistent with standard practice for near-zero carbohydrate protein foods. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. glycemic_index field is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not applicable for this negligible-carbohydrate food, with gi_category explicitly set to 'not_applicable' to clarify interpretation.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (113 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (242 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Raw skinless turkey breast is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium content and moderate potassium level support blood pressure regulation, but patients should avoid high-sodium marinades, brines, or sauces during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.14 g/100 g) and high protein (23.66 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw skinless turkey breast is an excellent protein source for individuals with type 2 diabetes; its near-zero carbohydrate content produces no glycaemic response, and its high protein content promotes satiety and supports lean muscle mass without raising blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.41 g/100 g) and low total fat (1.48 g/100 g) with high lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw skinless turkey breast is a heart-healthy lean protein choice; its very low saturated fat content supports LDL cholesterol management, and its high protein-to-fat ratio makes it preferable to higher-fat poultry or red meat cuts for cardiovascular disease risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of positive anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw skinless turkey breast does not worsen rheumatoid arthritis but provides no active anti-inflammatory benefit; patients should pair it with anti-inflammatory foods such as oily fish, leafy greens, or olive oil to achieve a more RA-supportive meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Turkey breast without skin, raw, is the uncooked pale-pink lean muscle meat from the breast portion of a domestic turkey (Meleagris gallopavo), with the skin fully removed. It is one of the leanest animal protein sources available, containing only 1.48 g of total fat and 0.41 g of saturated fat per 100 g, while delivering an exceptionally high protein content of 23.66 g per 100 g. The raw meat has a firm, slightly moist texture and a very mild, neutral flavour that readily absorbs marinades, herbs, and spices during cooking. It is typically sold as a whole breast, a split half-breast, or as boneless fillets. Common cooking methods include roasting, grilling, pan-searing, poaching, and slow-cooking. Nutritionally, raw skinless turkey breast is notable for its very low carbohydrate content (0.14 g/100 g), making it suitable for ketogenic, low-carbohydrate, and diabetic dietary patterns. Its low sodium content (113 mg/100 g) and moderate potassium (242 mg/100 g) make it appropriate for hypertension management. The meat is rich in B vitamins, particularly niacin (9.93 mg/100 g) and vitamin B6 (0.92 mg/100 g), and provides meaningful selenium (27.6 mcg/100 g) and zinc (1.61 mg/100 g). It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is recommended as a lean protein for weight management, cardiovascular disease prevention, and blood glucose control in type 2 diabetes. It is widely consumed across Western cuisines and is a staple protein in health-conscious and sports-nutrition dietary contexts globally.

Is skinless turkey breast good for weight loss?

Is turkey breast low in fat?
Can I eat turkey breast if I have high blood pressure?

Is turkey breast healthier than chicken breast?
How do I cook skinless turkey breast?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171494), both approved primary sources. The food is a well-defined, standardised raw ingredient with consistent nutrient profiles across approved databases. Additional micronutrient values drawn directly from USDA SR Legacy.
notes: Dietary fibre is 0.0 g per 100 g: confirmed zero per HPB and USDA — raw turkey breast meat contains no dietary fibre as it is an animal muscle tissue product (naturally zero). Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for this cut. Vitamin C is 0.0 mg and Vitamin A is 0.0 mcg RAE: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — raw turkey breast contains no meaningful vitamin C or preformed vitamin A. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.14 g/100 g); glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value with gi_category explicitly 'not_applicable'. Monounsaturated fat (0.27 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.39 g), and all micronutrient values not present in the HPB input were sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 171494. Halal status noted as true for plain raw turkey breast; consumers should verify halal certification for specific commercial products. Kosher status is not confirmed as it depends on slaughter method and certification.
